title: A barrel of rotting apples: Political corruption in modern Britain
gist: A Prospect podcast episode summary previews an interview with anti-corruption academic Robert Barrington, who argues UK corruption is more widespread than assumed across politicians, prisons, the monarchy and the Met, applying a four-part corruption test to cases from the Boris Johnson era, Grenfell, and the Thames Water sewage crisis ahead of his book 'Corrupted Kingdom.'

insights.claim: Robert Barrington, professor of anti-corruption practice at Sussex University's Centre for the Study of Corruption, argues that corruption in Britain is more widespread than commonly assumed, spanning politicians, prisons, the monarchy, and the Metropolitan Police.
insights.speaker: Prospect Team
insights.so_what: Sets a broad corruption-risk framing across UK public institutions beyond politics — relevant to any UK institutional-integrity or governance-risk screening.
insights.quote: corruption is more widespread than assumed in Britain, from politicians to prisons, the monarchy to the Met.

insights.claim: Barrington applies a four-part test for corruption to UK institutional conduct and discusses the 'good chap theory' of British governance — the assumption that officials behave honorably without needing strict formal rules — alongside misconduct from the Boris Johnson era.
insights.speaker: Prospect Team
insights.so_what: Gives a reusable framework for evaluating whether informal-norm-based UK governance is still fit for purpose — relevant heading into a leadership transition.
insights.locator: Robert discusses the Boris Johnson era misconduct

themes.theme: Corporate scandals as corruption case studies

insights.claim: The episode analyzes corporate scandals including Grenfell and the Thames Water sewage crisis through Barrington's corruption framework.
insights.speaker: Prospect Team
insights.so_what: Links regulatory and corporate-governance failures (Thames Water, Grenfell) to a broader institutional-corruption thesis — relevant to utilities-sector regulatory-risk and infrastructure-liability monitoring.

insights.claim: Barrington's book 'Corrupted Kingdom: Britain's Disappearing Integrity—And How We Can Get It Back' is due to be published by Profile Books on 16 July 2026.
insights.speaker: Prospect Team
insights.so_what: Flags an upcoming publication likely to shape UK political-integrity discourse ahead of a leadership transition — worth monitoring for media and political-cycle impact.
insights.locator: will be published by Profile Books

insights.claim: With a new UK prime minister on the cards, the episode raises open questions about what practical steps could be taken to reduce corruption risk going forward.
insights.speaker: Prospect Team
insights.so_what: Positions UK institutional-integrity reform as a live policy question tied to the next leadership transition — relevant to UK political-risk monitoring.
insights.locator: with a new prime minister on the cards
